description: When no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients is configured and the client MAC is not learned then the first L2VPN DHCP client packet is dropped.

When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.
Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.
For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.
No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

feature: IGMP/PIM on 6-in-6, Loopback and GRE interfaces
description: IGMP cannot be enabled on either Loopback or GRE interfaces. IGMP and PIM is not supported on a 6-in-6 Tunnel.

Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.
WAN MACsec with a custom ether type will not be established between CX devices when there are PVOS switches in the bypass.

In an environment with Cisco devices, when the GCM-AES-XPN-128 or GCM-AES-XPN-256 cipher suite is used for establishing the MACsec channel, the MKA policy on the Cisco device must be configured with ssci-based-on-sci.
In an environment with a Cisco device, the Cisco device must be designated as the key server. Designating the AOS-CX as the key server results in complete traffic loss.

MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.
Passing multicast traffic over GRE, 6in4 or 6in6 tunnels is not supported.
REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.
Stateful services for VRFs, where route leaking is enabled, are not supported.
BFD is not supported on a sub-interface. A sub-interface as underlay for EVPN-VXLAN is not supported
When configuring GRE, 6in4 and 6in6 tunnels with the underlay as a static route, the next-hop IP should be an SVI or ROP IP and not configured as the Active-Gateway.

Upgrading 9300/9300S devices from a build earlier than 10.15 to any release later than 10.15 causes the VXLAN interface configuration to be lost if the VXLAN interface is configured in a device with the Spine profile.
VXLAN configuration is not supported on the 9300/9300S platform when using spine or DC-Optimized-Spine profiles.
A GRE tunnel in an access-VLAN underlay is not supported in a non-default VRF.

Overlay/system partition information is included in HOST-RESOURCES-MIB polling. The overlay partition is a read-only partition and this is reflected in SNMP queries. hrFSAccess.3 = readOnly(2) is expected behavior for overlay partitions. Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

Symptom: Policy hitcounts commands using the vrf specifier do not include policies applied to L3 VNIs associated with the specified VRF. Scenario: This issue may occur when configuring an L3 VNI with a VRF, applying a policy to it, and sending traffic to increment hitcounts. Attempting to show policy hitcounts for the VRF fails and a report that no policy is applied to the VRF. Attempting to clear hitcounts for the VRF has no effect. Workaround: When showing or clearing hitcounts for all applications associated with the VRF, use a separate command to show or clear hitcounts for each L3 VNI.
symptom: Policy hitcounts commands using the vrf specifier do not include policies applied to L3 VNIs associated with the specified VRF.
scenario: This issue may occur when configuring an L3 VNI with a VRF, applying a policy to it, and sending traffic to increment hitcounts. Attempting to show policy hitcounts for the VRF fails and a report that no policy is applied to the VRF. Attempting to clear hitcounts for the VRF has no effect.
workaround: When showing or clearing hitcounts for all applications associated with the VRF, use a separate command to show or clear hitcounts for each L3 VNI.

description: Symptom: RPVST BPDU counter statistics stay at zero and do not increment. Scenario: This issue may occur after clearing spanning-tree statistics or when switching between STP modes. Workaround: Reboot the switch, toggle RPVST on and off, or change the RPVSY priority.
symptom: RPVST BPDU counter statistics stay at zero and do not increment.
scenario: This issue may occur after clearing spanning-tree statistics or when switching between STP modes.
workaround: Reboot the switch, toggle RPVST on and off, or change the RPVSY priority.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.
symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets.
scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header.
workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

description: Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.
symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation.
scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication.
workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

description: Symptom: Mirror session becomes unresponsive to CLI commands (cannot be modified or deleted) while remaining active in hardware. Scenario: This issue may occur when an internal database reconnection occurs while a mirror session is active. This can be triggered by a service restart, or transient connectivity issue. Workaround: Perform a warm reboot on the switch.
symptom: Mirror session becomes unresponsive to CLI commands (cannot be modified or deleted) while remaining active in hardware.
scenario: This issue may occur when an internal database reconnection occurs while a mirror session is active. This can be triggered by a service restart, or transient connectivity issue.
workaround: Perform a warm reboot on the switch.

description: Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.
symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast.
scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length.
workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

description: Symptom: BGP peer group member sessions do not reset when the include-TCP-options setting is modified, while individual peer sessions reset as expected. This creates inconsistent behavior between peer group and standalone peer configurations. Scenario: This issue occurs when modifying or removing the include-TCP-options setting on a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured. Individual peer sessions respond correctly to the configuration change by resetting, but peer group member sessions remain established without resetting.
symptom: BGP peer group member sessions do not reset when the include-TCP-options setting is modified, while individual peer sessions reset as expected. This creates inconsistent behavior between peer group and standalone peer configurations.
scenario: This issue occurs when modifying or removing the include-TCP-options setting on a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured. Individual peer sessions respond correctly to the configuration change by resetting, but peer group member sessions remain established without resetting.

description: Symptom: BGP session with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) remains in Established state even when a keychain key mismatch exists between peers. The session does not reset when the matching key is deleted from one peer, and manual session reset using clear bgp \* does not resolve the issue. This creates a security vulnerability as the session continues operating with mismatched authentication parameters. Scenario: This issue occurs when a matching keychain key is deleted from one BGP peer after both peers have been configured with the same key. The deletion creates a parameter mismatch between the two peers (different send-id, recv-id, or key string values), but the BGP session fails to detect this mismatch and remains established instead of resetting.
symptom: BGP session with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) remains in Established state even when a keychain key mismatch exists between peers. The session does not reset when the matching key is deleted from one peer, and manual session reset using clear bgp \* does not resolve the issue. This creates a security vulnerability as the session continues operating with mismatched authentication parameters.
scenario: This issue occurs when a matching keychain key is deleted from one BGP peer after both peers have been configured with the same key. The deletion creates a parameter mismatch between the two peers (different send-id, recv-id, or key string values), but the BGP session fails to detect this mismatch and remains established instead of resetting.

description: Symptom: Attempts to unconfigure a keychain that is currently being used by a routing protocol (BGP with TCP-AO), result in the system displaying a confirmation message warning that the action disrupts protocol adjacencies. However, after confirming the removal by entering yes, the keychain is not actually deleted and remains in the configuration. Scenario: This issue occurs when trying to remove a keychain that is actively associated with a BGP neighbor configured with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO). The system presents a warning dialog and requests user confirmation, but fails to execute the deletion command even after confirmation is provided.
symptom: Attempts to unconfigure a keychain that is currently being used by a routing protocol (BGP with TCP-AO), result in the system displaying a confirmation message warning that the action disrupts protocol adjacencies. However, after confirming the removal by entering yes, the keychain is not actually deleted and remains in the configuration.
scenario: This issue occurs when trying to remove a keychain that is actively associated with a BGP neighbor configured with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO). The system presents a warning dialog and requests user confirmation, but fails to execute the deletion command even after confirmation is provided.

description: Symptom: When BGP graceful restart is triggered on a TCP-AO authenticated BGP peer, the peer router does not mark the received routes as stale (indicated by the S prefix). Instead, routes are immediately removed from the routing table, causing a temporary loss of traffic visibility and convergence delay until the restarting peer comes back online. Scenario: This issue occurs when performing a graceful restart (redundancy switchover or hpe-routing restart) on a BGP peer that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with keychain authentication. The peer router fails to enter graceful restart helper mode and does not preserve routes with stale markers during the restart window.
symptom: When BGP graceful restart is triggered on a TCP-AO authenticated BGP peer, the peer router does not mark the received routes as stale (indicated by the S prefix). Instead, routes are immediately removed from the routing table, causing a temporary loss of traffic visibility and convergence delay until the restarting peer comes back online.
scenario: This issue occurs when performing a graceful restart (redundancy switchover or hpe-routing restart) on a BGP peer that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with keychain authentication. The peer router fails to enter graceful restart helper mode and does not preserve routes with stale markers during the restart window.

description: Symptom: BGP peer group sessions with TCP-AO configured remain in Established state even after the include-TCP-options parameter is removed on one side. The sessions do not reset as expected, and they remain up even after manual session reset using clear bgp \*. Scenario: This issue occurs when the include-TCP-options setting is removed from a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with a keychain. While one side removes the parameter, the peer group member sessions on both sides continue to remain established instead of tearing down due to the configuration mismatch.
symptom: BGP peer group sessions with TCP-AO configured remain in Established state even after the include-TCP-options parameter is removed on one side. The sessions do not reset as expected, and they remain up even after manual session reset using clear bgp \*.
scenario: This issue occurs when the include-TCP-options setting is removed from a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with a keychain. While one side removes the parameter, the peer group member sessions on both sides continue to remain established instead of tearing down due to the configuration mismatch.

content: Refer to the Approved Product Lists sites for the Common Criteria, FIPS 140-2 and DoDIN APL to obtain the product certification details. Products should be used as evaluated and defined in the respective configuration guides. Common Criteria: <https://www.niap-ccevs.org/Product/> FIPS 140-2: <https://csrc.nist.gov/Projects/Cryptographic-Module-Validation-Program/Validated-Modules/Search> DoDIN APL: <https://aplits.disa.mil/processAPList.action>

description: Symptom: ISL connectivity may be affected as the full MTU override has not been applied to some ports. VSX connectivity between peers may be affected. Scenario: This issue may occur during bring up of ISL ports and may result in MTU override not being set correctly during port configuration. Ports that were missed during this configuration remain missed, even if more ports are added to ISL later. Workaround: Fully unconfigure the ISL and affected ports then reconfigure for proper MTU override to be applied.
symptom: ISL connectivity may be affected as the full MTU override has not been applied to some ports. VSX connectivity between peers may be affected.
scenario: This issue may occur during bring up of ISL ports and may result in MTU override not being set correctly during port configuration. Ports that were missed during this configuration remain missed, even if more ports are added to ISL later.
workaround: Fully unconfigure the ISL and affected ports then reconfigure for proper MTU override to be applied.

description: Symptom: BFD sessions on ROP flap continuously and never come up. Scenario: This issue may occur if ROP uses the same destination IP on multiple VRFs. As a result, BFD sessions using those IPs never come up. Workaround: Configure the IPs on a VLAN/SVI. Using the same destination IP on multiple VRFs on VLANs bring the BFD session up.
symptom: BFD sessions on ROP flap continuously and never come up.
scenario: This issue may occur if ROP uses the same destination IP on multiple VRFs. As a result, BFD sessions using those IPs never come up.
workaround: Configure the IPs on a VLAN/SVI. Using the same destination IP on multiple VRFs on VLANs bring the BFD session up.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.
symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet.
scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool.
workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

description: Symptom: Routes take up to two minutes to switch to an alternate path when an active BGP neighbor is disabled, leading to delays in traffic rerouting. Scenario: This issue occurs in a network utilizing BGP routing. When a BGP session is disabled on one switch, the corresponding routes remain in a stale state until the stale path timer expires, causing traffic loss during the transition.
symptom: Routes take up to two minutes to switch to an alternate path when an active BGP neighbor is disabled, leading to delays in traffic rerouting.
scenario: This issue occurs in a network utilizing BGP routing. When a BGP session is disabled on one switch, the corresponding routes remain in a stale state until the stale path timer expires, causing traffic loss during the transition.
